title: Deep cleaning a rabbit colony pen
subtitle: Stripping and disinfecting the colony pen to break parasite and pathogen cycles.
excerpt: A deep clean of the colony pen removes accumulated parasite eggs, coccidial oocysts, and bacterial contamination that routine spot-cleaning misses. Done twice a year, and whenever there is an outbreak of coccidiosis or gut disease, it resets the pen's disease burden significantly.
sourceNotes: RWAF colony housing and hygiene guidance, rabbitwelfare.co.uk. British Rabbit Council husbandry notes. FAO Small-scale rabbit production manual (2009).

glossaryTerms.definition: The hardy egg-like stage of Eimeria, the parasites causing coccidiosis. Oocysts survive for weeks on dry surfaces and months in moist soil. Disinfectants that destroy most pathogens do not reliably destroy oocysts.
The period a pen is left empty and dry after cleaning and disinfection. Exposing the cleaned surfaces to sunlight and dry air reduces oocyst viability more effectively than disinfectant alone.

content.text: Routine spot-cleaning keeps a pen habitable but does not break the disease cycle. Parasite eggs and gut bacteria build up in bedding and soil that daily cleaning leaves behind. A deep clean, done twice yearly and after any disease outbreak, strips the pen back to bare surfaces and lets it dry out before restocking.
are the hardest to remove; drying and sunlight help more than disinfectant alone.

House the colony in temporary quarters for the duration of the clean, a large cage or a clean section of the run. Rabbits can tolerate temporary confined housing for two to three days without significant stress if they have hay, water, and a companion.
Remove all bedding, loose hay, and manure with a muck fork. Bag and remove from the pen area entirely.
Scrape the floor and walls to remove any dried manure or caked bedding. Plastic scrapers work on wire mesh; a flat spade on a solid floor.
Scrub all surfaces with hot water and a stiff brush. Pay attention to corners, the bases of wire panels, the underside of nest boxes, and the lips of feeding bowls.
Apply a disinfectant designed for rabbit housing. Products with quaternary ammonium compounds work against bacteria and many viruses. Follow the dilution and contact time on the label.
Rinse well. Disinfectant left on surfaces irritates the eyes, nose, and lungs of the rabbits.
Leave to dry completely, preferably two to three days in dry weather with good airflow. Sun exposure on damp surfaces is the most effective additional step against oocysts.

Oocysts are not killed by standard disinfectants. The best tool against them is ammonia solution (dilute household ammonia in water) applied after the primary disinfectant and rinsed off, followed by sunlight and a dry period of at least forty-eight hours. This is not a perfect control but reduces the oocyst burden significantly.

is the period the pen sits empty before restocking. Two to three days in good weather is the minimum. A pen that has been through a coccidiosis episode benefits from a longer downtime of five to seven days in dry sunny conditions, particularly for the soil-floored areas of a colony run.
Before returning the rabbits, bed the pen deeply with fresh hay or straw and check all water points and feeders are clean and functioning.
